title: ServiceAccounts, Tokens, and kubectl auth
description:
Go beyond basic RBAC bindings to understand ServiceAccount token security, testing permissions before deploying, and advanced patterns like aggregated ClusterRoles.

## Overview
Once you understand the four RBAC resources, the next challenge is operating them safely. The most common cluster security breaches involve over-permissioned ServiceAccounts or tokens that are unnecessarily mounted into Pods. This unit covers how to harden SA usage and how to audit permissions without deploying real workloads.

## The Default ServiceAccount Risk
Every namespace has a `default` ServiceAccount. By default, every Pod that does not specify `serviceAccountName` runs as `default`. In older Kubernetes versions, the `default` SA had broad read access. Even in modern clusters with restricted defaults, developers often add ClusterRole bindings to `default` for convenience — then every Pod in the namespace inherits that access.

Best practice: **never bind permissions to the default SA**. Always create named ServiceAccounts per application.

## automountServiceAccountToken
Kubernetes automatically mounts the SA's API token into every Pod at `/var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/token`. If your Pod never calls the Kubernetes API, this token is a security liability — any container escape exposes it.

Disable auto-mounting at the ServiceAccount level (affects all Pods using it) or per Pod:

```yaml
# On the ServiceAccount (preferred)
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: my-app-sa
automountServiceAccountToken: false
---
# Or per Pod
spec:
  automountServiceAccountToken: false
  serviceAccountName: my-app-sa
```

## kubectl auth can-i
Test permissions before deploying. This is essential for CI/CD pipelines and debugging.

```bash
# Can my current user create deployments in staging?
kubectl auth can-i create deployments -n staging

# Can ServiceAccount ci-sa in staging delete pods?
kubectl auth can-i delete pods --as system:serviceaccount:staging:ci-sa -n staging

# List all permissions a subject has
kubectl auth can-i --list --as system:serviceaccount:staging:ci-sa -n staging
```

## Impersonation for Testing
Cluster admins can impersonate any user or SA to test their view without creating real tokens:

```bash
kubectl get pods --as system:serviceaccount:default:monitor-sa
kubectl get secrets --as system:serviceaccount:default:monitor-sa  # should fail
```

## Aggregated ClusterRoles
Aggregate multiple ClusterRoles into one using label selectors. The aggregated role automatically picks up new ClusterRoles that match the selector — useful for extensible permission sets:

```yaml
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRole
metadata:
  name: monitoring-aggregate
aggregationRule:
  clusterRoleSelectors:
  - matchLabels:
      rbac.example.io/aggregate-to-monitoring: "true"
rules: []  # auto-populated from matching ClusterRoles
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRole
metadata:
  name: pod-metrics-reader
  labels:
    rbac.example.io/aggregate-to-monitoring: "true"
rules:
- apiGroups: ["metrics.k8s.io"]
  resources: ["pods"]
  verbs: ["get", "list"]
```

## Common Pitfalls
- **Leaving automountServiceAccountToken enabled**: any container escape in the Pod exposes the SA token to the attacker. Disable for all Pods that don't call the Kubernetes API.
- **Wildcard verbs (`*`) or resources (`*`)**: convenient but dangerous. Grants create, delete, escalate for everything. Even cluster-admin should be reserved for break-glass access.
- **ClusterRoleBinding for namespace work**: if a CI/CD SA only needs to deploy to `staging`, bind it with a RoleBinding in `staging` — not a ClusterRoleBinding.
- **Forgetting to bind the SA**: the SA exists, the Role exists, but there is no RoleBinding — all requests return 403. Always verify with `kubectl auth can-i`.

quizzes.question: What is the security risk of leaving automountServiceAccountToken enabled on a Pod that does not call the Kubernetes API?

options.text: The Pod uses more CPU because it refreshes the token periodically
A container escape exposes the SA token, which an attacker can use to call the Kubernetes API with the SA's permissions
The token expires after 24 hours and causes the Pod to restart
The Pod cannot access external networks when the token is mounted

quizzes.question: Which kubectl command checks whether ServiceAccount ci-sa in namespace staging can delete pods in that namespace?

options.text: kubectl auth can-i delete pods -n staging
kubectl auth can-i delete pods --as system:serviceaccount:staging:ci-sa -n staging
kubectl get rolebinding -n staging | grep ci-sa
kubectl describe serviceaccount ci-sa -n staging

quizzes.question: Why is granting permissions to the default ServiceAccount dangerous?

options.text: The default SA cannot be used for RoleBindings — it is reserved for system components
Every Pod in the namespace that does not specify serviceAccountName automatically runs as the default SA, inheriting those permissions
The default SA token is stored in plaintext in etcd
Kubernetes ignores bindings on the default SA for security reasons

quizzes.question: What does an aggregationRule in a ClusterRole do?

options.text: It merges the ClusterRole with a Role from a specific namespace
It automatically populates the ClusterRole's rules from other ClusterRoles that match a label selector
It limits the ClusterRole to aggregate (read-only) operations
It combines multiple RoleBindings into a single ClusterRoleBinding

quizzes.question: Where is the ServiceAccount token automatically mounted inside a Pod?

options.text: /etc/kubernetes/token
/var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/token
/run/serviceaccount/token
Tokens are injected as environment variables, not files

quizzes.question: A CI/CD ServiceAccount needs to deploy only to the staging namespace. Which binding minimizes the blast radius?

options.text: ClusterRoleBinding so the SA can act across all namespaces if needed later
RoleBinding in the staging namespace referencing a ClusterRole or Role with deployment permissions
ClusterRole with namespace: staging in the metadata
No binding is needed — CI/CD tools bypass RBAC by default

quiz_explanations.q1: The SA token mounted in a Pod is a valid credential for the Kubernetes API. If an attacker gains code execution inside the container (container escape, SSRF, etc.), they can read the token file and make authenticated API calls with the SA's permissions. Disabling automounting eliminates this attack surface for Pods that don't need API access.
quiz_explanations.q2: kubectl auth can-i supports --as to impersonate a subject. The format for impersonating a ServiceAccount is --as system:serviceaccount:<namespace>:<name>. This tests the actual RBAC policies without needing the SA's token.
quiz_explanations.q3: Kubernetes assigns the default ServiceAccount to any Pod that omits spec.serviceAccountName. If you bind permissions to default, every Pod in that namespace — including those from third-party Helm charts and accidentally deployed workloads — inherits those permissions. Always use per-application ServiceAccounts.
quiz_explanations.q4: aggregationRule makes a ClusterRole a 'meta-role' whose rules are automatically merged from any ClusterRole matching the label selector. This is used by built-in roles like admin, edit, and view to be extensible by CRD operators without modifying the core roles.
quiz_explanations.q5: Kubernetes mounts three files under /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/: token (JWT), ca.crt (cluster CA), and namespace (current namespace string). Client libraries like client-go automatically discover these files when running inside a cluster.
quiz_explanations.q6: A RoleBinding in the staging namespace scopes the SA's permissions to that namespace only. Even if the ClusterRole has broad rules, the RoleBinding confines them to staging. A ClusterRoleBinding would give the SA those permissions in every namespace, which violates the principle of least privilege.
